passages.explanation: Luke opens with a formal prologue addressed to 'most excellent Theophilus' — likely a patron or Roman official. He explains his method: many have undertaken to compile an account of the events fulfilled among us, handed down by eyewitnesses and servants of the word. Luke has carefully investigated everything from the beginning and is writing an orderly account so that Theophilus may know the certainty of the things he's been taught. This is the most explicit statement of historical method in the New Testament. Luke isn't claiming divine dictation — he's claiming thorough research.

Luke introduces Zechariah, a priest of the division of Abijah, and his wife Elizabeth, a descendant of Aaron. Both are righteous before God, walking blamelessly in all the Lord's commands. But they have no children — Elizabeth is barren and they're both very old. The setup echoes Abraham and Sarah, and other barren-couple stories in the Old Testament. God's biggest moves often begin with an impossible birth.

While Zechariah is serving in the temple — chosen by lot to burn incense, a once-in-a-lifetime honour — an angel of the Lord appears to him, standing at the right side of the altar of incense. Zechariah is terrified. The angel tells him not to be afraid: his prayer has been heard. Elizabeth will bear a son, and he's to name him John. He'll be a joy and delight, and many will rejoice at his birth. He'll be great in the Lord's sight, never drink wine or fermented drink (a Nazirite vow), and be filled with the Holy Spirit even before birth. He'll turn many of Israel's children to the Lord their God, going before the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah — turning the hearts of parents to their children and the disobedient to the wisdom of the righteous. He'll prepare a people made ready for the Lord. This is the announcement of John the Baptist's birth — the forerunner prophesied in Malachi.

Zechariah asks the angel: how can I be sure of this? I'm an old man and my wife is well along in years. The angel answers: I am Gabriel. I stand in the presence of God, and I've been sent to tell you this good news. And now you'll be silent and unable to speak until the day this happens, because you didn't believe my words. Meanwhile the people outside are wondering why Zechariah is taking so long in the temple. When he comes out, he can't speak and keeps making signs to them. They realise he's seen a vision. He finishes his term of service and goes home. Elizabeth becomes pregnant and remains in seclusion for five months, saying: the Lord has done this for me — he has shown his favour and taken away my disgrace among the people. Zechariah's silence is both a sign and a consequence — doubt gets disciplined.

In the sixth month of Elizabeth's pregnancy, God sends Gabriel to Nazareth in Galilee, to a virgin pledged to be married to a man named Joseph, of the house of David. The virgin's name is Mary. Gabriel greets her: you who are highly favoured, the Lord is with you. Mary is deeply troubled and wonders what kind of greeting this is. The angel tells her not to be afraid: she's found favour with God. She will conceive and give birth to a son, and she's to call him Jesus. He will be great and called the Son of the Most High. The Lord God will give him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over Jacob's descendants forever — his kingdom will never end. Gabriel's announcement draws directly from the Davidic covenant in 2 Samuel 7. The child will be both a human descendant of David and the eternal Son of God.

Mary asks: how will this be, since I am a virgin? Gabriel explains: the Holy Spirit will come on you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you. So the holy one to be born will be called the Son of God. Even Elizabeth your relative is going to have a child in her old age — she who was said to be unable to conceive is in her sixth month. For no word from God will ever fail. Mary's response: I am the Lord's servant. May your word to me be fulfilled. The contrast with Zechariah is striking — he asked for proof and was struck mute. Mary asks a practical question and receives an answer. Her faith isn't blind; it's willing.

Mary hurries to a town in the hill country of Judea and enters Zechariah's home to visit Elizabeth. When Elizabeth hears Mary's greeting, the baby leaps in her womb, and Elizabeth is filled with the Holy Spirit. She cries out: blessed are you among women, and blessed is the child you will bear! But why am I so favoured, that the mother of my Lord should come to me? As soon as your greeting reached my ears, the baby in my womb leaped for joy. Blessed is she who has believed that the Lord would fulfil his promises to her. Elizabeth recognises what's happening before anyone tells her — the Spirit reveals it. John's ministry of pointing to Jesus begins in the womb.

Mary responds with a song — the Magnificat. My soul glorifies the Lord, my spirit rejoices in God my Saviour. He has been mindful of the humble state of his servant. From now on all generations will call me blessed, because the Mighty One has done great things for me. His mercy extends to those who fear him, from generation to generation. He has scattered the proud, brought down rulers from their thrones, and lifted up the humble. He has filled the hungry with good things but sent the rich away empty. He has helped his servant Israel, remembering to be merciful to Abraham and his descendants forever. Mary stays with Elizabeth about three months and then returns home. The Magnificat echoes Hannah's prayer in 1 Samuel 2 — another woman whose impossible child changed Israel's history. Mary's song is revolutionary: God inverts power structures, exalts the lowly, and remembers his covenant.

Elizabeth's time comes and she gives birth to a son. Her neighbours and relatives rejoice. On the eighth day they come to circumcise the child and want to name him after his father. Elizabeth insists: he is to be called John. They object — no one in the family has that name. They make signs to Zechariah. He asks for a writing tablet and writes: his name is John. Everyone is astonished. Immediately Zechariah's mouth is opened, his tongue is freed, and he begins to speak, praising God. Fear falls on the whole neighbourhood, and throughout the hill country of Judea people discuss what has happened. Everyone asks: what then is this child going to be? For the Lord's hand was with him. Zechariah's obedience — naming the child as the angel commanded — breaks the silence. The nine months of muteness end with worship.

Zechariah is filled with the Holy Spirit and prophesies — the Benedictus. He praises God for visiting and redeeming his people, raising up a horn of salvation in the house of David, as he spoke through his holy prophets long ago — salvation from enemies, mercy to the ancestors, remembering his holy covenant sworn to Abraham. Then he turns to his son: you, child, will be called a prophet of the Most High. You will go before the Lord to prepare his ways, to give his people knowledge of salvation through the forgiveness of their sins. Because of God's tender mercy, the rising sun will come to us from heaven, to shine on those living in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the path of peace. The child grows, becomes strong in spirit, and lives in the wilderness until he appears publicly to Israel. Zechariah's prophecy splits into two parts — the coming King and the coming prophet. His son will prepare the way for a salvation already set in motion.
